title: Frustrated neurons: Energy landscapes and relaxation dynamics in repulsive phase oscillators
abstract: Geometrical frustration, a central paradigm in condensed matter physics, provides a unifying language for systems in which locally preferred interactions cannot be made globally compatible. Here, we use this language to formulate a minimal theory of frustrated neural timing, mapping repulsively coupled rhythmic units onto antiferromagnetic XY models. Within this framework, the condensed-matter concepts of local constraints, degenerate ground-state manifolds, metastability, and quench dynamics become a concrete diagnostic framework for structured neural phase dynamics. We analyze a hierarchy of geometries: a triangle as the minimal frustrated motif with two chiral 120° timing states, a tetrahedron whose reduced ground-state manifold consists of intersecting continuous branches associated with antipodal pairings, and a kagome lattice on which local constraints define a constrained three-coloring manifold. The kagome lattice reveals the central dynamical result: zero-temperature relaxation suppresses global synchrony but typically selects low-energy metastable torque-balanced states rather than exact ground states. Finally, we show how the phase theory can be carried back towards biophysical neural models by treating it as an effective-interaction target, where geometrical timing frustration is realized through preferred phase lags that become incompatible around closed motifs. This perspective suggests that weak global coherence in neural systems does not necessarily signal disordered activity, but can reflect structured local timing order shaped by a frustrated dynamical landscape.
summary: This work translates the concept of geometrical frustration from condensed matter physics into a minimal theory for neural timing. By mapping repulsive phase oscillators to an antiferromagnetic XY model, the authors show that local timing incompatibilities create structured energy landscapes. This suggests that weak global coherence in the brain may be a signature of complex, locally ordered dynamics rather than mere desynchronization.
main_problem: To develop a minimal theoretical framework that maps the concept of geometrical frustration, central to condensed matter physics, onto the dynamics of repulsively coupled rhythmic units in neural systems.
main_result: The paper shows that weak global coherence in neural systems can reflect structured local timing order dictated by a frustrated dynamical landscape, rather than indicating simple disorder.
method: The authors use the language of antiferromagnetic XY models and analyze the energy landscapes and relaxation dynamics across a hierarchy of geometries (triangle, tetrahedron, kagome lattice).
model_or_system: The model consists of repulsively coupled phase oscillators, mathematically analogous to the XY model Hamiltonian, where local constraints (anti-synchronizing phase preferences) are incompatible on non-bipartite motifs.
key_observables: Local chirality ($\chi_	riangle$), mean bond frustration ($ ar{f}$), basin probabilities ($p_A$), and the Kuramoto order parameter ($R$).
important_parameters: Temperature ($T$) (noise strength), coupling strength ($K$), and the geometry of the lattice (e.g., triangle, kagome).
main_assumptions: The theory relies on a minimal phase-oscillator limit, neglecting amplitude dynamics, synaptic delays, and assuming symmetric coupling.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the $120^\circ$ pattern on a triangle and the general energy landscape analysis for various frustrated geometries.
paper_structure: The paper establishes a dictionary mapping magnetic concepts to neural timing analogs, derives the phase energy function for the XY model, and then analyzes the relaxation dynamics across increasing complexity of frustrated geometries.
why_interesting: The use of established tools from frustrated magnetism (e.g., ground-state manifolds, metastable states) to diagnose collective behavior in neural oscillators provides a powerful, non-equilibrium diagnostic framework.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Antiferromagnetic Ising frustration on a triangle. Fixing two neighboring spins to satisfy one bond leaves the third spin unable to be antiparallel to both, so at least one bond must remain unsatisfied.

FIG. 2. Schematic hierarchy of frustrated phase-oscillator geometries studied in Sec. IV. (a) A two-oscillator pair provides the unfrustrated baseline: a single repulsive bond can be satisfied exactly by an antiphase relation. (b) The triangular motif is the minimal geometrically frustrated unit, where three repulsive bonds cannot all be satisfied simultaneously, so the reduced ground states form two chiral 120◦configurations. (c) The tetrahedral motif introduces a continuous reduced ground-state manifold, organized by antipodal pairings of planar spins. (d) A small kagome lattice extends the triangular constraint to a network of corner-sharing triangles, producing a many-state constrained...

FIG. 4. Two-oscillator baseline for a single phase-repulsive bond. (a) The laboratory-frame phases θi, shown modulo 2π, continue to rotate while approaching a fixed antiphase separation. Passing to the rotating-frame variables ϕi = θi − ωt removes the common drift without changing the relative phase, δ = θ1 −θ2 = ϕ1 −ϕ2. (b) The one-bond energy E = K cos δ decreases toward its minimum value at δ = π, while the bond-frustration measure f12 = (1+cos δ)/2 relaxes to zero, indicating a fully satisfied antiphase bond.

FIG. 3. Reduced energy-landscape description of the two- oscillator phase-repulsive bond. (a) The one-bond energy E(δ) = K cos δ has unstable in-phase maxima at δ = 0 and 2π, and a stable antiphase minimum at δ = π. Arrows indi- cate the direction of the reduced gradient flow in the relative phase δ. (b) Relative-phase trajectories from many initial conditions converge to the antiphase state δ = π. The ex- actly in-phase state is unstable and remains fixed only for the fine-tuned initial condition δ = 0 mod 2π.

FIG. 5. Reduced energy landscape and gradient-flow dynamics of the frustrated triangular motif. The global phase degree of freedom is removed by working in the relative rotating-frame coordinates (x, y) = (ϕ1 −ϕ3, ϕ2 −ϕ3). (a) Three-dimensional surface plot of the energy E/K = cos(ϕ1 −ϕ2) + cos(ϕ2 −ϕ3) + cos(ϕ3 −ϕ1) expressed on the relative-phase plane. The two red points mark the degenerate 120◦minima, corresponding to the two possible chiral arrangements of the phases. (b) Contour plot of the same landscape with white energy contours and green gradient-flow trajectories from many initial conditions. The flow relaxes toward one of the two chiral minima, labeled by χ△= ±1. The cyan square...
